property.title: What property qualifies for Montenegro residency?
property.text: Since January 17, 2026, temporary residency can only be obtained by purchasing property with a tax (cadastral) value of at least €150,000. This requirement applies to new applications. The property must be registered in Montenegro's cadastre, with ownership registered to the applicant.

duration.title: Do I need to live in Montenegro permanently after obtaining residency?
duration.text: Yes. The new version of the Foreigners Act requires residency holders to actually live in Montenegro. A long absence may become grounds for refusing to renew the permit. Before traveling outside the country, it's worth accounting for the current limits on absence and the requirements of the migration authorities.

taxes.title: Can I obtain residency for my spouse and children?
taxes.text: Yes. Once residency is granted, the main applicant can obtain a residence permit for their spouse and minor children through the family reunification program. This requires supporting documents proving the family relationship, along with meeting the legal requirements.

permanent.title: Can I work with property-based residency?
permanent.text: No. Residency obtained on the basis of property ownership does not grant the right to be employed in Montenegro. If you plan to formally work or run a business, you'll need to arrange the corresponding basis for residence and work.

residencyTime.title: What happens if I sell the property?
residencyTime.text: Selling the property means losing the basis on which residency was granted. If you don't establish another lawful basis for residence before the permit expires, you won't be able to renew it.

permamentResidence.title: Can I obtain permanent residency after property-based residency?
permamentResidence.text: No. Under the current legislative changes, time spent under residency obtained on the basis of property ownership does not count toward the period required for permanent residency. If you plan to obtain permanent residency later, you'll need to hold residency under a basis that counts toward the required period.

time.title: How long does residency processing take?
time.text: After the property ownership is registered and a complete set of documents is filed, review of the application usually takes 30–60 days. The actual timeline depends on the completeness of the documents and the workload of the competent authority.

possibilityToDecline.title: Can residency be refused?
possibilityToDecline.text: Yes, if the applicant does not meet the legal requirements or submits an incomplete document package. The most common reasons for refusal are the property not meeting the established requirements, errors in the documents, entry restrictions, or failure to meet the conditions of stay in Montenegro. A preliminary legal review of the documents and the property significantly reduces the risk of refusal.

eligibility.title: Who can obtain residency by opening a company or registering a sole proprietorship?
eligibility.text: Foreign nationals who have registered a company (DOO) or a sole proprietorship (Preduzetnik) in Montenegro and meet the requirements of the Foreigners Act can obtain residency. To apply, you need to register the business, meet the migration law requirements, and provide a complete set of documents.

ownershipShare.title: Do I need to own the entire company to obtain residency?
ownershipShare.text: No. You don't need to be the sole founder of the company to obtain residency. However, the new legal requirements set separate conditions for owners of 51% or more of the company's shares, as well as for executive directors, when renewing residency.

realActivity.title: Does the company need to conduct genuine business activity?
realActivity.text: Yes. The company must carry out genuine business activity, file its accounting and tax reports on time, and fulfill all obligations to the state. A company that is registered but not active can become grounds for refusing to renew residency. When renewing, foreign nationals who are executive directors or own more than 51% of the company must confirm that the company paid at least €5,000 in taxes and mandatory social contributions over the previous year. This requirement does not apply to the same extent to citizens of EU, EEA, and Swiss states, for whom the law provides a special regime.

renewalRequirements.title: What requirements apply when renewing residency through a company?
renewalRequirements.text: When renewing residency, owners of more than 51% of the company and executive directors must confirm that the company paid at least €5,000 in taxes and mandatory social contributions over the previous year. This is one of the key requirements of the new version of the law.

residencyTime.title: Do I need to live in Montenegro permanently?
residencyTime.text: Yes. As with other bases for residency, the law requires actually living in Montenegro. Failing to meet the requirements for staying in the country can become grounds for refusing to renew the residence permit.

family.title: Can I obtain residency for family members?
family.text: Yes. Once residency is granted, the main applicant can obtain a residence permit for their spouse and minor children through the family reunification program, provided the legal requirements are met.

permanent.title: Can I obtain permanent residency after business-based residency?
permanent.text: Yes. Unlike property-based residency, time spent under residency through a company or sole proprietorship counts toward the period required for permanent residency, provided the legal requirements are met and residence is continuous and lawful.

processingTime.title: How long does it take to obtain residency through a company or sole proprietorship?
processingTime.text: After the company or sole proprietorship is registered and the complete set of documents is prepared, the residency application is usually reviewed within 30–60 days. The timeline may vary depending on the completeness of the documents and the workload of the competent authorities.

eligibility.title: Who can obtain Digital Nomad residency in Montenegro?
eligibility.text: Foreign nationals who work remotely for a foreign employer, own a company registered outside Montenegro, or provide services to foreign clients can obtain this residency. The main condition is that the income source must be located outside Montenegro.

workProof.title: How do I prove remote work?
workProof.text: To file the application, you need to provide documents proving remote employment or business activity outside Montenegro. This can include an employment contract, a contract with a foreign company, documents on registering your own business, client contracts, bank statements, and other documents confirming the source of income.

residencyTime.title: Do I need to live in Montenegro permanently?
residencyTime.text: Yes. As with other bases for residency, a Digital Nomad status holder must comply with the legal requirements on actually living in Montenegro. Failing to meet the residence rules may affect the ability to renew the temporary residence permit.

family.title: Can I obtain residency for my spouse and children?
family.text: Yes. Once residency is granted, the main applicant can obtain a residence permit for their spouse and minor children through the family reunification program, provided the legal requirements are met.

permanent.title: Can I obtain permanent residency after Digital Nomad status?
permanent.text: No. Under current legislation, residency obtained through the Digital Nomad program is a special type of temporary residence and does not count toward the period required for permanent residency. If you plan to obtain permanent residency later, you'll need to hold residency under a basis that counts toward the required period.

duration.title: What's the validity period of Digital Nomad residency?
duration.text: Temporary residency for digital nomads is issued for up to two years. After it expires, it can be renewed for up to another two years if the applicant still meets the program's requirements.

processingTime.title: How long does residency processing take?
processingTime.text: After a complete set of documents is filed, the application is reviewed by the competent authorities. Processing typically takes 30 to 60 days, though the timeline may depend on the completeness of the documents and the current workload of the migration service.

possibilityToDecline.title: Why might a Digital Nomad residency application be refused?
possibilityToDecline.text: A Digital Nomad residency application may be refused if the applicant fails to prove remote work and sufficient income, submits an incomplete document package, provides inaccurate information, or if there are legal obstacles to residing in Montenegro. The main reason for refusals is insufficient proof of the basis for residency.

workRight.text: All adult family members (spouses and older children) who obtain residency through reunification have automatic and full rights to legally work in Spain.

income.title: Minimum income from €2,849/month
income.text: For 2026, the income requirement for the main applicant is at least 200% of the Spanish minimum wage (SMI). Income must come from remote activity outside Spain.

allowance.title: Supplement for family members
allowance.text: For a joint filing, the financial threshold increases: +75% of the SMI (around €1,069) for a spouse, and +25% (around €356) for each child.

statement.title: Income stability
statement.text: Income must be regular and confirmed by bank statements for the past 3 months. It must come from official invoices or payslips.

savings.title: Savings on account (additional factor)
savings.text: We recommend showing a free balance in your personal bank account that covers your family's living expenses for several months ahead, as proof of financial independence.

cleanRecordDoc.title: Legalization and apostilles
cleanRecordDoc.text: All official documents issued outside Spain (diplomas, marriage/birth certificates, criminal record certificates) must carry the international "Apostille" stamp.

civilDocs.title: Sworn translation (Traducción Jurada)
civilDocs.text: Documents must be translated into Spanish exclusively by a sworn translator (traductor jurado) accredited by the Spanish Ministry of Foreign Affairs. Standard translations are not accepted.

notary.title: Proof of 3+ years of experience
notary.text: You must prove your qualification either through a diploma in a relevant field or a detailed CV together with a work record or past contracts covering the last 3 years.

incomeProof.title: Permission to work from Spain
incomeProof.text: A key document is an official letter of permission from your employer, explicitly stating the company's consent for you to perform your duties remotely from Spain.

incomeRequirements.title: What are the income requirements for Digital Nomad residency in Spain?
incomeRequirements.text: The minimum income for Digital Nomad residency in Spain is tied to the Spanish minimum wage (SMI), and in 2026 stands at at least €2,849 per month for the main applicant. If you're relocating with family, the financial requirements increase: an additional +75% of the SMI (around €1,069) for a spouse, and a further +25% (around €356) for each child. Income must be confirmed by official contracts, foreign bank statements, and tax returns.

whoCanGet.title: Who can get the Digital Nomad visa in Spain?
whoCanGet.text: Spain's Digital Nomad visa is designed for remote employees of foreign companies (on staff), as well as freelancers, sole proprietors, and self-employed individuals whose business or clients are registered outside Spain. The main requirements for a nomad residency applicant are a relevant higher education degree (or at least 3 years of confirmed work experience in their field) and a contract with an employer or clients signed at least 3 months prior.

requiredDocuments.title: What documents are needed for Digital Nomad residency in Spain?
requiredDocuments.text: The full list of documents for nomad residency includes: an international passport, a remote work contract, a permission letter from the employer to work from Spain, a foreign bank statement for 3 months with payslips, an apostilled certificate of no criminal record, and an apostilled relevant diploma. All documents not in Spanish require mandatory official sworn translation in Spain (Traducción Jurada).

processingTime.title: How long does the Spanish nomad residency application take to review?
processingTime.text: The official review period for Digital Nomad residency filed within Spain is 20 business days. In practice, the entire process of preparing the file, obtaining sworn translations, filing, and waiting for a decision from the Migration Ministry (UGE) takes about 1.5–2 months. After the application is approved, you just need to schedule fingerprinting and pick up your plastic TIE resident's card.

familyApplication.title: Can I get Spain's Digital Nomad residency for the whole family?
familyApplication.text: Yes, the program allows nomad residency in Spain to be obtained for the whole family within a single joint filing. You can include a spouse (including a confirmed cohabiting partner without formal marriage), children up to age 26 (if enrolled full-time and financially dependent on you), and dependent parents. All adult family members automatically gain the right to work in Spain.

taxation.title: What taxes does a digital nomad pay in Spain?
taxation.text: Once you become a Spanish tax resident, a Digital Nomad residency holder can apply for a special favorable regime — the "Beckham Law" (Ley Beckham). This regime allows you to pay a flat income tax (IRPF) at a reduced rate of 15% on income up to €600,000 per year, instead of the standard progressive scale (which can reach 47%). This makes taxes for digital nomads in Spain some of the most attractive in Western Europe.

stayRequirement.title: Do I need to live in Spain permanently to keep my residency?
stayRequirement.text: The law does not set a strict requirement to spend more than 183 days a year in the country to keep your Digital Nomad residency. However, if your long-term goal is permanent residency in Spain after 5 years or citizenship after 10 years, you need to actually live in the country. For permanent residency, your absences must not exceed 10 months total across all 5 years.

consulateVsInside.title: What's the difference between applying for the visa at a consulate versus filing for residency inside Spain?
consulateVsInside.text: If you apply through a Spanish consulate in your country of citizenship/residence, you're issued a Digital Nomad visa for 1 year. If instead you enter Spain on a Schengen visa and file the documents online from within the country, you receive Digital Nomad residency for 3 years right away. The vast majority of our clients choose to file from within Spain, as it grants long-term status without an intermediate renewal.

localCompanies.title: Can I work for Spanish companies with Digital Nomad residency?
localCompanies.text: Yes, the law allows residency holders to work in the local market, but with a strict limit: your income from Spanish clients or companies must not exceed 20% of your total revenue. The bulk of your income (at least 80%) must come from foreign clients outside Spain. If you plan to work exclusively for local Spanish business, this program won't suit you.

prAndCitizenship.title: How do I obtain permanent residency and citizenship in Spain through the nomad visa?
prAndCitizenship.text: Immigrating to Spain through the Digital Nomad program is a direct and fully legal path to a European passport. Time spent in the country under nomad status counts fully toward residency time. After 5 years of continuous residence, you become eligible for permanent residency (EU long-term residence), and after 10 years of legal residence and passing language and culture exams, you can apply for Spanish citizenship.

ipremCalc.title: How do I calculate the required amount under IPREM for Spain in 2026?
ipremCalc.text: Migration law requires demonstrating passive income of at least 400% of the Spanish IPREM indicator for the main applicant, plus 100% for each dependent family member. Amounts are indexed annually by the government. Besides monthly income, approval also requires showing liquid funds in a bank account — from €30,000 for the first resident card, to demonstrate absolute financial solvency.

rentalIncome.title: Does rental income qualify for non-lucrative residency in Spain?
rentalIncome.text: Yes, income from long-term leasing of residential or commercial property is one of the most reliable and well-accepted forms of passive income for the Spanish Consulate. The key condition is providing an officially registered lease agreement, a certificate of ownership (registry extract), and bank statements confirming regular and lawful receipt of rent over the past 3 years.

renewalDocs.title: What documents are needed to renew Spanish No Lucrativa residency after a year?
renewalDocs.text: For the first renewal (issued for 2 years at once), you'll need to prove you met the residency requirement of staying in Spain for at least 182 days. You'll also need to show updated statements from a Spanish bank (this time, the balance must cover 2 years of residence — meaning at least €60,000 for the main applicant), a valid private health insurance policy with no copayments, and confirmation of no outstanding tax debts.

schoolRequirement.title: Will I need to enroll my children in a local school when applying for financially independent residency?
schoolRequirement.text: Yes, if you're applying for Spanish residency for the whole family and have school-age children (6 to 16), the Consulate strictly enforces compulsory education law. The document package for children must include official proof (matrícula) of enrollment or a reserved place in a public, semi-public (concertado), or private Spanish school.

dividendsIncome.title: Do dividends from a business in Russia or the CIS qualify as passive income?
dividendsIncome.text: Dividends from owning shares in a business or stock in major companies qualify for this immigration program. For a successful case, you'll need to provide the company's tax returns, an official shareholder meeting resolution on dividend payments for the past 3 years, income tax certificates (or their local equivalents), and bank records proving the actual receipt of these funds into your personal account.

wealthTax.title: Do I need to pay property and worldwide income tax with No Lucrativa residency?
wealthTax.text: Since this type of residency requires you to spend more than 183 days a year in the kingdom, you automatically become a Spanish tax resident. This means you're obligated to declare and pay taxes on your worldwide income (including profits from business, rent, and investments outside Spain). We recommend a preliminary tax consultation with Aurion to lawfully structure and optimize your assets before filing.

bankAccountRemote.title: Can I open a Spanish bank account remotely for immigration purposes?
bankAccountRemote.text: Today, opening a Spanish bank account entirely remotely without a personal visit or a notarized power of attorney to a qualified Spanish lawyer is practically impossible due to strict KYC and AML (anti-money-laundering) protocols. Aurion's experts help clients remotely prepare and legalize a compliance document package for Spanish compliance departments, so the account-opening process goes quickly and without refusals.

rejectionReasons.title: What are the main reasons for refusal of non-lucrative residency in Spain?
rejectionReasons.text: The most common reasons for refusal are an unclear origin of funds, the consul's doubts about the "passive" nature of the income (for example, if hidden employment is suspected), or an insufficient bank account balance. Another common mistake is submitting a cheap health insurance policy with a copayment (copago) instead of a full annual policy. Aurion's lawyers conduct a thorough audit of every case, fully eliminating such risks before filing.

rentOutProperty.title: Does No Lucrativa residency allow me to buy and rent out property in Spain?
rentOutProperty.text: Yes, despite not being able to work as an employee or run an operating business, this status doesn't restrict you from investing. You have the full legal right to buy residential or commercial property in Spain, register it in your name, and officially rent it out, earning lawful passive income into your Spanish bank account.

goldVisaDiff.title: How does non-work residency differ from Spain's Golden Visa investor program?
goldVisaDiff.text: The main difference is the financial entry threshold and obligations. The "Golden Visa" requires a non-refundable property investment from €500,000, but doesn't require you to live in the country permanently (no 183-day requirement). Non-work residency (No Lucrativa) doesn't require investment at all — renting housing and showing savings is enough — but it does require you to spend more than half the year in Spain and become its tax resident.

minPriceRequirements.title: What's the minimum property value in Serbia for obtaining residency?
minPriceRequirements.text: Serbian law sets no minimum property value for Serbia residency. You can buy any residential apartment or house, whether on the secondary market or a new build. The main requirement is that the property is officially registered in the cadastre and fit for permanent habitation, since the police can verify that you actually live at the address.

hiddenThreshold.title: Is there a hidden minimum property value threshold not written into the law?
hiddenThreshold.text: Officially, Serbia's Foreign Nationals Act sets no value limit — you can buy a modest cottage for €15,000 and file your documents. In practice, however, there's an unwritten filter applied by the MUP: the property must be fit for full, permanent habitation. If you buy a derelict village house with no utilities (electricity, water, heating) or a tiny share in an apartment, a migration service inspector will come to check (and they do visit in person to verify residence) and refuse the residency, citing the property as purchased purely for documentation purposes (a sham basis). We recommend focusing on genuinely livable apartments or move-in-ready houses.

commercialRealEstate.title: Does buying commercial property qualify for Serbia residency?
commercialRealEstate.text: No, the law clearly states that the basis for Serbia residency is buying residential property specifically (a house or apartment). Commercial premises (offices, warehouses, shops) do not grant residency rights under this program. If you want to invest in the commercial sector, it's better to use the "company registration (DOO)" basis instead.

taxObligations.title: Is a foreigner required to pay taxes when buying property, and how does it affect residency?
taxObligations.text: When buying property, an investor is subject to taxes, and payment receipts may be checked during residency renewal. When buying on the secondary market, an absolute rights transfer tax (Porez na prenos apsolutnih prava) of 2.5% of the assessed value is paid. When buying a new-build apartment from a developer entity, VAT (PDV) of 10% is paid instead (the 2.5% tax does not apply in this case). Providing a registered purchase agreement automatically triggers the taxation process.

rentalPossibility.title: Can I rent out the purchased property while keeping my residency?
rentalPossibility.text: Absolutely not. The basis for this type of residency is specifically "ownership of property in which the foreigner personally resides." If a police inspector, during a routine check, finds that other people (tenants) are living in the apartment while the owner is renting elsewhere or absent from the country, the residency will be revoked on the spot. Renting out is only possible if the owner switches to a different residency basis (for example, opening a sole proprietorship) or has already obtained permanent residency (after 3 years).

reciprocityAndCitizenship.title: Can any Russian/Belarusian citizen buy property in Serbia? How does the reciprocity principle work?
reciprocityAndCitizenship.text: Foreigners can acquire property in Serbia only where a reciprocity principle (Ugovor o reciprocitetu) exists between the countries. Serbia has a full reciprocity agreement with Russia and Belarus — citizens of these countries can buy apartments and houses without restriction. An important limitation for all foreigners: buying agricultural land in Serbia is prohibited (farmland, forests, border zones). A foreigner may only own the plot on which their residential house directly stands (plus the surrounding yard/grounds), but not a farm field.

workAuthorization.title: Does property-based residency grant the right to work in Serbia?
workAuthorization.text: Yes. Since 2024, Serbia has had a Unified Permit law (Jedinstvena dozvola) combining residency and work permits. When obtaining property-based residency, the applicant and their family members (who obtained status through reunification) have the right to apply for work authorization without needing to open a legal entity or obtain an invitation from an employer. This makes buying property a highly advantageous alternative to opening a sole proprietorship if your goal is simply to be employed by a Serbian or international company.

prAndPassport.title: Can I obtain permanent residency and Serbian citizenship through property?
prAndPassport.text: Buying a home is a direct path to permanent status. You can obtain Serbian permanent residency through property after 3 years of continuous residence in the country under residency status. It's important to meet the residence requirements: you can be absent from Serbia for no more than 6 consecutive months and no more than 10 months total across the entire 3-year period. After obtaining permanent residency, you have the right to apply for Serbian citizenship.

incomeRequirements.title: Can Russian citizens open a bank account in Serbia in 2026?
incomeRequirements.text: Yes, Serbia remains one of the few European countries where a Russian citizen can freely open a bank account. For personal needs, an account is opened once you have a "white card" (registration); for business, it's opened after officially registering a sole proprietorship or DOO in the APR registry. Local Serbian banks offer multi-currency accounts (RSD, EUR, USD) to Russian clients, support incoming and outgoing international SWIFT transfers, and issue Visa and Mastercard cards that work worldwide.

whoCanGet.title: How do I open a sole proprietorship in Serbia to obtain residency?
whoCanGet.text: To open a sole proprietorship in Serbia for residency purposes (the so-called Preduzetnik status), you need to file an application with Serbia's Business Registers Agency (APR). This can be done either in person upon arrival or remotely via power of attorney through Aurion's lawyers. After registering the business in Serbia, you open a checking account at a bank and can immediately file an online application for a unified residence and work permit.

requiredDocuments.title: What is the "white card" in Serbia, and how do I get it?
requiredDocuments.text: The "white card" in Serbia (beli karton) is a mandatory initial registration document showing a foreigner's temporary place of stay. It must be arranged within 24 hours of crossing the Serbian border. If you're staying at a hotel or hostel, the administration registers you automatically. If you're renting an apartment or buying your own home, you need to visit the local police department (MUP) together with the property owner, or fill out an online form through the government services portal to register as a foreigner in Serbia.

processingTime.title: How does residency renewal work in Serbia?
processingTime.text: The Serbian residency renewal procedure (Privremeni boravak) is done entirely online through the unified permits portal. It's recommended to file your renewal application no earlier than 30 days before your current status expires. For a successful renewal, you'll need to confirm your basis is still valid (provide a current APR extract for a sole proprietorship, or a current property sheet for homeowners), show funds in a Serbian account, and provide valid health insurance.

familyApplication.title: Can I obtain residency for the family of a business resident?
familyApplication.text: Yes, the spouse and minor children of the main applicant have the right to obtain residency on the basis of family reunification. Documents can be filed either at the same time as the main applicant, or after they receive their resident's ID card. An important benefit is that reunited family members automatically gain the right to legally work in Serbia.

taxation.title: What is the tax patent (Paušal) in Serbia, and who does it suit?
taxation.text: The Paušal is a special tax regime in Serbia with a fixed monthly tax rate that doesn't require complex bookkeeping or filing returns. It's ideal for freelancers, developers, and consultants earning up to 6 million dinars a year (~€50,000). The exact tax amount for a sole proprietorship in Serbia is calculated individually by the tax service and depends on the chosen activity code and the municipality where the business is registered.

stayRequirement.title: What are the mandatory-stay requirements for business owners in Serbia?
stayRequirement.text: There's no strict requirement to spend more than 183 days a year in the country to renew business-based residency. However, if your goal is Serbian permanent residency after 3 years, you need to live in the country almost continuously. Absences of no more than 10 months total over the entire three-year period, or no more than 6 months per single trip, are permitted.

consulateVsInside.title: Do I need to pay taxes when buying an apartment in Serbia?
consulateVsInside.text: When purchasing housing, a foreign buyer takes on certain tax obligations. If you buy on the secondary market, an absolute rights transfer tax (Poreza na prenos apsolutnih prava) of 2.5% of the assessed value is paid. If you buy a new-build apartment from a developer, VAT (PDV) of 10% is paid instead (usually already included in the property price). Notary and sworn translator fees are also paid when signing the agreement.

localCompanies.title: What are the real costs of maintaining property in Serbia?
localCompanies.text: Serbia attracts immigrants with low living and housing maintenance costs compared to the EU. Monthly utility bills in Belgrade (heating, electricity, water, waste collection) for a standard 50–60 sq. m apartment average from €80 to €150 (depending on the season). Serbia's annual property tax is calculated individually based on the property's size and location, and typically ranges from €100 to €300 per year.

prAndCitizenship.title: Can I obtain Serbian permanent residency through business in 3 years?
prAndCitizenship.text: Yes, Serbia offers one of the fastest paths to permanent residency in Europe. By running an active business and paying taxes on time, you're entitled to apply for Serbian permanent residency through business after just 3 years of continuous residence in the country under residency status. Permanent residency status fully exempts you from having to renew Serbian residency annually and opens a direct path to citizenship.

overview.title: A working path to residency without buying property
overview.firstText: Residency through business or sole proprietorship registration in Armenia is a reliable and fast basis for entrepreneurs and remote professionals who want to establish themselves legally in the country and operate on the international market.
overview.secondText: No property purchase or large investment is required — a housing lease agreement and a registered business are enough. We register the business, open accounts, and fully manage the case through to the residency card. For renewal, the company must show minimal activity and pay taxes. After 3 years of continuous residence under this status, you gain the right to apply for Armenian citizenship.

roadmap.title: From arrival to the residency card — step by step
roadmap.additionalInfo: The process shown is for a typical case of business/sole proprietorship registration in Armenia.

steps.title: I. Consultation and Strategy
steps.text: We break down your situation, choose the optimal business form (sole proprietorship with simplified taxation, or LLC), and calculate the tax burden before you start. We set a realistic timeline.

steps.title: II. Business Registration
steps.text: We register the sole proprietorship or legal entity in Armenia's State Registry, obtain a taxpayer ID, arrange a social card (SSN), open accounts at a local bank, and help with a lease agreement.

steps.title: III. Application Filing
steps.text: We prepare the complete document package, including medical certificates and certified notarized translations. We submit the case to Armenia's Migration Service.

steps.title: IV. Receiving the Card and Settling In
steps.text: You pass a security check and, within 30–45 days, receive your plastic Armenian residency card. We help you get set up with your first reporting and taxes.

steps.title: V. Taxes and Renewal
steps.text: We help with bookkeeping, paying minimal taxes, and renewing your residency status on time. After 3 years of continuous residence, you gain the right to apply for citizenship.

incomeRequirements.title: Do I need to show real turnover on my sole proprietorship's account for residency approval?
incomeRequirements.text: Yes, Armenia's Migration Service and National Security Service (NSS) now strictly check that businesses are genuine. To be approved, you need to show movement in your sole proprietorship's bank account. It's recommended to demonstrate turnover or a balance of at least 1,000,000 AMD (about $2,600) on the business account over the 60 days before filing, as well as have active contracts with clients.

whoCanGet.title: Who is eligible for Armenian residency through sole proprietorship registration?
whoCanGet.text: Any foreign national aged 18 or older who has registered a sole proprietorship (or opened an LLC) in Armenia and conducts genuine business activity can obtain temporary residency. There are no citizenship restrictions for most countries. The main requirements are legal entry into the country, no legal issues, and the ability to demonstrate business activity.

requiredDocuments.title: What documents should I prepare in my home country before flying to Armenia?
requiredDocuments.text: For the main applicant, an international passport is enough (valid for at least 1.5–2 years). If you plan to file documents for family members, be sure to bring the original marriage certificate and children's birth certificates. These documents need an Apostille (or legalization, depending on the issuing country). All translations into Armenian and notarization are done locally, in Armenia.

processingTime.title: How long does the turnkey Armenian residency process take?
processingTime.text: The entire process, from your arrival in Yerevan to receiving your plastic residency card, takes on average 1.5 to 2 months (45–60 days). Registering the sole proprietorship, obtaining the taxpayer ID and social card (SSN), and opening accounts takes 5–7 business days. Review of the residency application by the Migration Service and the NSS check take up to 30–45 days.

familyApplication.title: Can I bring my family to Armenia under business residency?
familyApplication.text: Yes, the main applicant's family members (spouse, minor children, and in some cases financially dependent parents) have the full right to obtain residency through the family reunification procedure. Documents for relatives can be filed right after the main applicant (the sole proprietorship owner) receives their plastic residency card.

taxation.title: What taxes does a sole proprietor pay in Armenia when applying for residency?
taxation.text: Most IT professionals, marketers, and freelancers choose the turnover tax (similar to a simplified tax regime), with a rate from 1.5% to 5% (depending on the field of activity). There are also fixed social and military contributions (around $25–$40 per month). For certain types of IT activity, upon obtaining a special certificate, the income tax rate can be reduced to 0%, making Armenia one of the most attractive tax havens in the CIS.

stayRequirement.title: Do I need to buy or rent property to obtain residency?
stayRequirement.text: Buying property in Armenia is not required for this type of residency. However, you'll need a registration address in Armenia. An official apartment lease agreement (certified at a notary or the cadastre), or the owner's consent for you to register at their address at the passport office, will work. We help resolve this as part of our support.

consulateVsInside.title: What's the validity of business residency, and how do I renew it?
consulateVsInside.text: Initial temporary residency is issued for 1 year. To renew it for another year, you'll need to file a similar document package 30 days before the card expires, and prove that your sole proprietorship continued operating, filed its reports on time, and paid taxes into Armenia's budget over the past year.

localCompanies.title: Can I open a bank account in Armenia without residency, just with a sole proprietorship?
localCompanies.text: Yes, a registered sole proprietorship is solid grounds for opening a business account and a personal account at Armenian banks (such as ID Bank, Ameriabank, Acba Bank, and others). Banks run a compliance (KYC) process, checking your contracts and sources of funds. Having an open account with activity on it becomes a key argument when later filing for residency.

prAndCitizenship.title: Does Armenian residency lead to citizenship?
prAndCitizenship.text: Yes. Under Armenian law, a foreign national who legally and continuously resides in the country under residency status for 3 years has the right to apply for Armenian citizenship. This also requires passing a basic test on knowledge of the Armenian Constitution, in the Armenian language.

overview.title: Key Parameters

The process has been fully digitized since 2022, eliminating bureaucracy and long lines at migration centers.
overview.secondText: You receive a single plastic card (Work Permit + Residence Card), granting the right to legally live, work, and access medical and financial services in Armenia. To keep your status, you simply need to remain employed by the company — the employer pays your income tax and social contributions.

roadmap.title: Process Roadmap
roadmap.additionalInfo: The process is digitized and runs through the official portal of Armenia's Migration Service.

0.title: Consultation and Employer Verification
0.text: We analyze your employment contract. We check the employer company for compliance with migration law requirements. We register the company's profile on the workpermit.am portal.

1.title: Publishing the Vacancy and Documents
1.text: The employer posts the vacancy on the platform for a labor market check. Meanwhile, we help the employee arrange their social card (SSN), pass the medical exam, and get certified document translations.

2.title: Electronic Filing and NSS Review
2.text: We upload the employee's complete document package to the system. The application goes for approval to the Ministry of Labor, the Migration Service, and Armenia's NSS. The entire process is tracked online.

3.title: Receiving the Unified Card
3.text: Once the application is approved, you sign the final employment contract, and the authority issues a single plastic card that serves as both a work permit and residency. We collect the card in Yerevan.

cardDefinition.title: What is the unified employment residency card in Armenia?
cardDefinition.text: It's a single plastic document that combines both an official work permit and temporary residency. It confirms your right to legally stay in the country and work for a specific employer.

eaecRequirement.title: Are EAEU citizens required to obtain a work permit?
eaecRequirement.text: No, citizens of EAEU countries (Russia, Belarus, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan) are exempt from obtaining a standard work permit. However, they and their employers are still required to register the employment contract on the workpermit.am portal to confirm their legal residency status.

employerChange.title: Can I change employers while holding a work-based residency?
employerChange.text: Yes, but your current residency is tied to a specific employer. Upon termination, the old residency is revoked. You'll have a limited time to find a new employer, who must re-initiate your registration process on the workpermit.am platform.

stateFees.title: What state fees apply to work-based residency?
stateFees.text: The state fee for reviewing the application and issuing an employment-based residency card is 105,000 AMD (about $270) per year. The fee is usually paid by the employer or the employee themselves at the time of filing.

diplomaRequirements.title: What are the requirements for an employee's education diploma?
diplomaRequirements.text: For some positions, the Migration Service requests proof of qualification. The diploma must be translated into Armenian and notarized. For citizens of certain countries, apostille or consular legalization of the diploma may be required.

nssCheck.title: Does an employed applicant go through an NSS screening?
nssCheck.text: Yes. Armenia's National Security Service checks all foreign nationals filing for residency (including employees). The check runs in the background after the documents are submitted through the online portal.

remoteWork.title: Can I get residency while working remotely for a foreign company?
remoteWork.text: Work-based residency is only issued with a contract from an Armenian legal entity (branch, representative office, or local company). If you work remotely for a company in another country, residency through registering your own sole proprietorship in Armenia would suit you better.

validityPeriod.title: What's the validity period of work-based residency?
validityPeriod.text: Temporary employment-based residency is issued for the duration of your employment contract, but no more than 1 year. Each year, as the employment contract is renewed, the residency is renewed for another year through the same online platform.

terminationRisk.title: Can my employer fire me and cause me to lose my residency?
terminationRisk.text: Yes, in the event of contract termination, the employer must notify the Migration Service via the workpermit.am portal. After that, your work permit and residency are revoked. You'll need to either find a new job, switch to a different residency basis (for example, opening a sole proprietorship), or leave the country.

bankAccount.title: Does work-based residency let me open an account at an Armenian bank?
bankAccount.text: Yes, having an official employment contract in Armenia and approved work-based residency significantly simplifies the bank compliance process. You'll be able to easily open personal accounts in dram, dollars, or euros at the country's leading banks.

We handle the whole process: from a full audit of the developer and running a secure transaction, to filing documents with Greece's Migration Ministry. The property can be legally rented out for passive income in euros.

roadmap.title: From property selection to the residency card — step by step.
roadmap.additionalInfo: The process shown is for a typical property purchase case in Greece.

steps.title: Consultation and Property Selection
steps.text: We analyze your goals. We select property under the new thresholds (€250k / €400k / €800k). Our lawyers verify the property's legal standing.

steps.title: Opening Accounts and the Transaction
steps.text: We obtain a Greek tax number (AFM) and open bank accounts. We handle the payment and sign the final purchase agreement.

steps.title: Filing the Application (Blue Slip)
steps.text: We prepare the document package and file the application online. The investor receives a temporary residence permit (Blue Slip) allowing legal presence in Greece.

steps.title: Fingerprinting and Receiving the Card
steps.text: Within 6 months, we travel to Greece for fingerprinting. Within 1.5–2 months, we collect the completed plastic residency cards, valid for 5 years.

minInvestment.title: What's the minimum investment amount for the Golden Visa?
minInvestment.text: Since autumn 2024, the minimum threshold is €800,000 in premium zones (Athens, Thessaloniki, Mykonos, Santorini) and €400,000 in other regions. However, a discounted option of €250,000 remains available when purchasing commercial properties for conversion into residential use, or when restoring architectural landmarks.

stayRequirement.title: Do I need to live in Greece permanently?
stayRequirement.text: No. There is no mandatory residence requirement. You can be absent from the country entirely and still retain your resident status.

rentPossibility.title: Can I rent out the property I purchased?
rentPossibility.text: Yes, you have the full right to rent out the property (either long-term or short-term via platforms like Airbnb) and earn income in euros.

workRight.title: Does the Golden Visa grant the right to work in Greece?
workRight.text: The Golden Visa doesn't grant the right to be employed in Greece, but you can own a business, set up companies, and receive dividends.

citizenshipPath.title: Can I obtain Greek citizenship through the Golden Visa?
citizenshipPath.text: Yes, but this requires actually living in Greece for at least 7 years, paying taxes there, and passing a language and culture exam.

cardValidity.title: How long is the residency card valid for?
cardValidity.text: The card is issued for 5 years right away. It can be renewed an unlimited number of times, every 5 years, as long as the property remains in your ownership.
